concepts.title: Physical Nature of Matter
concepts.definition: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. It is made up of very small particles which are continuously moving.
examples: Sugar crystals disappear when dissolved in water, showing tiny particles mixing with water.
The smell of perfume spreads in a room due to motion of particles.

concepts.title: States of Matter
concepts.definition: Matter mainly exists in three states: solid, liquid and gas, differing in inter‑particle distances, forces of attraction and freedom of movement.
examples: Ice is solid with fixed shape and volume; water is liquid with fixed volume but variable shape; steam is gas with neither fixed shape nor volume.

concepts.title: Characteristics of Solids, Liquids and Gases
concepts.definition: Solids have definite shape and volume; liquids have definite volume but take shape of container; gases have neither definite shape nor volume and are highly compressible.
examples: A wooden block keeps its shape; water takes shape of glass; LPG cylinder shows high compressibility of gas.

concepts.title: Change of State: Melting, Boiling and Sublimation
concepts.definition: Matter can change from solid to liquid and liquid to gas on heating at fixed temperatures called melting point and boiling point; some solids change directly from solid to gas (sublimation).
examples: Ice melts at 0°C to water; water boils at 100°C at normal pressure.
Camphor and naphthalene sublime on heating.

concepts.title: Effect of Temperature and Pressure on State
concepts.definition: Increasing temperature increases kinetic energy and can change state; increasing pressure can liquefy gases by bringing particles closer.
examples: Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) is gas compressed under high pressure to become liquid in cylinders.

concepts.title: Evaporation and Factors Affecting It
concepts.definition: Evaporation is the surface phenomenon where liquid changes into vapour below its boiling point; it depends on temperature, surface area, humidity and wind speed.
examples: Wet clothes dry faster on a hot, windy day due to faster evaporation.
Evaporation causes cooling, as in sweating.

concepts.title: Pure Substance and Mixture
concepts.definition: Pure substances have fixed composition and properties, such as elements and compounds. Mixtures contain two or more substances physically combined.
examples: Distilled water is a pure substance; air and salt solution are mixtures.

concepts.title: Types of Mixtures: Homogeneous and Heterogeneous
concepts.definition: Homogeneous mixtures have uniform composition throughout; heterogeneous mixtures have non‑uniform composition with visible different parts.
examples: Sugar in water is homogeneous; sand in water is heterogeneous.

concepts.title: Solutions, Suspensions and Colloids
concepts.definition: Solutions are homogeneous mixtures with very small solute particles; suspensions are heterogeneous with large particles settling on standing; colloids have intermediate particle size and show Tyndall effect.
examples: Salt solution is a true solution; muddy water is a suspension; milk is a colloid.

concepts.title: Concentration of Solutions (Basic)
concepts.definition: Concentration describes amount of solute dissolved in a given quantity of solvent or solution.
formulae: Mass% of solute = (mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100
examples: 10 g of salt in 90 g of water gives 10% salt solution by mass.

concepts.title: Separation Techniques (Intro)
concepts.definition: Mixtures can be separated by methods based on differences in properties such as particle size, solubility, boiling point and magnetic nature.
examples: Filtration, decantation, evaporation, distillation, chromatography and using a magnet.

concepts.title: Laws of Chemical Combination (Concept)
concepts.definition: Chemical reactions follow fixed laws like law of conservation of mass and law of constant proportions.
formulae: Law of conservation of mass: total mass of reactants = total mass of products
examples: In burning of magnesium in air, total mass of reactants equals mass of magnesium oxide formed plus remaining oxygen.

concepts.title: Atoms, Elements and Symbols
concepts.definition: Atoms are basic units of elements; an element has only one type of atom. Each element has a chemical symbol.
examples: H for hydrogen, O for oxygen, Na for sodium, Fe for iron.

concepts.title: Molecules of Elements and Compounds
concepts.definition: A molecule is a group of two or more atoms chemically combined. Molecules of elements have same type of atoms, while molecules of compounds have different types.
examples: O₂ and N₂ are molecules of elements; H₂O and CO₂ are molecules of compounds.

concepts.title: Writing Chemical Formulae (Intro)
concepts.definition: Chemical formula of a compound represents its composition using symbols and subscripts showing fixed ratios of atoms.
examples: Water is H₂O (2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom).
Carbon dioxide is CO₂ (1 carbon atom and 2 oxygen atoms).

concepts.title: Subatomic Particles
concepts.definition: Atoms contain protons and neutrons in a nucleus and electrons revolving around the nucleus.
examples: Proton has positive charge, neutron is neutral and electron has negative charge.

concepts.title: Atomic Number and Mass Number
concepts.definition: Atomic number is the number of protons in an atom; mass number is the sum of protons and neutrons in the nucleus.
formulae: Mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons
examples: For carbon‑12, Z = 6, A = 12; it has 6 protons and 6 neutrons.

concepts.title: Isotopes and Isobars
concepts.definition: Isotopes are atoms of same element with same atomic number but different mass numbers; isobars are atoms of different elements with same mass number but different atomic numbers.
examples: ¹²C, ¹³C and ¹⁴C are isotopes of carbon.
⁴⁰Ca and ⁴⁰Ar are isobars.

concepts.title: Bohr’s Model (Intro)
concepts.definition: According to Bohr’s model, electrons revolve around nucleus in fixed circular orbits (shells) with fixed energies.
examples: K, L, M shells for first, second, third energy levels.

concepts.title: Cell Theory and Types
concepts.definition: Cells are basic structural and functional units of life. All living organisms are made of cells, which may be unicellular or multicellular.
examples: Amoeba is unicellular; humans are multicellular.

concepts.title: Cell Structure
concepts.definition: Typical cells have a cell membrane, cytoplasm and nucleus; plant cells also have a cell wall and plastids.
examples: Onion peel cell shows cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm and nucleus.

concepts.title: Cell Organelles and Functions
concepts.definition: Organelles like nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus and vacuoles perform specific functions.
examples: Mitochondria release energy; chloroplasts carry out photosynthesis; vacuoles store water and substances.

concepts.title: Differences Between Plant and Animal Cells
concepts.definition: Plant cells have cell wall, large vacuole and plastids; animal cells usually lack these and often have smaller vacuoles.
examples: Cheek cells are animal cells without cell walls; onion cells have cell walls.

concepts.title: Tissues and Their Need
concepts.definition: Tissues are groups of similar cells performing a specific function. In multicellular organisms, tissues provide division of labour.
examples: Muscle tissue helps in movement, nervous tissue helps in conduction of impulses.

concepts.title: Plant Tissues: Meristematic and Permanent
concepts.definition: Meristematic tissues are actively dividing; permanent tissues are derived from meristematic cells and perform specialised functions.
examples: Apical meristem at tips of roots and shoots causes growth.
Xylem and phloem are complex permanent tissues for transport.

concepts.title: Animal Tissues: Epithelial, Connective, Muscular, Nervous
concepts.definition: Animal tissues are classified on structure and function into epithelial (covering), connective (support), muscular (movement) and nervous (control and coordination).
examples: Bone and blood are connective tissues; skeletal muscles move limbs; nerve cells transmit impulses.

concepts.title: Distance and Displacement
concepts.definition: Distance is total path length travelled; displacement is shortest straight‑line distance from initial to final position in a specific direction.
examples: Walking around a field once returns you to start: distance is field perimeter, displacement is zero.

concepts.title: Speed and Velocity
concepts.definition: Speed is distance travelled per unit time; velocity is displacement per unit time in a given direction.
formulae: Average speed = total distance / total time
Average velocity = total displacement / total time
examples: A car covering 60 km in 1 h has speed 60 km/h.
Running in a circle and returning to start gives non‑zero speed but zero average velocity.

concepts.title: Uniform and Non-Uniform Motion
concepts.definition: Uniform motion means equal distances are covered in equal time intervals; non‑uniform motion means unequal distances in equal intervals.
examples: A bus at constant 40 km/h is in uniform motion; in city traffic it is in non‑uniform motion.

concepts.title: Acceleration
concepts.definition: Acceleration is rate of change of velocity with time; it can be positive (speeding up) or negative (slowing down).
examples: If a bike’s speed increases from 10 m/s to 20 m/s in 5 s, acceleration is (20 − 10)/5 = 2 m/s².

concepts.title: Distance–Time and Velocity–Time Graphs (Qualitative)
concepts.definition: Graphical representation of motion helps visualise speed, velocity and acceleration using distance–time and velocity–time graphs.
examples: Straight sloping line in distance–time graph indicates uniform speed; slope gives speed.

concepts.title: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces
concepts.definition: Balanced forces acting on a body cancel each other and do not change its state of motion; unbalanced forces produce acceleration.
examples: Equal pushes from both sides on a box keep it at rest (balanced).
One‑sided push causes box to move (unbalanced).

concepts.title: Newton’s First Law (Inertia)
concepts.definition: An object remains at rest or in uniform straight‑line motion unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force; this tendency is called inertia.
examples: Passengers jerk forward when a bus stops suddenly due to inertia of motion.

concepts.title: Newton’s Second Law (Basic Relation)
concepts.definition: Rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force and occurs in same direction.
formulae: For constant mass: F = m × a
examples: Pushing a lighter cart gives more acceleration than pushing a heavier one with same force.

concepts.title: Newton’s Third Law
concepts.definition: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction acting on different bodies.
examples: When a person jumps from a boat, the boat moves backward.
Rocket moves up as gases are pushed downwards.

concepts.title: Universal Law of Gravitation
concepts.definition: Every two objects in the universe attract each other with a force directly proportional to product of their masses and inversely proportional to square of distance between them.
examples: Gravitational attraction between Earth and Moon keeps the Moon in orbit.

concepts.title: Free Fall and Acceleration due to Gravity
concepts.definition: When an object falls under the influence of Earth’s gravity alone, it is in free fall with nearly constant acceleration g near Earth’s surface.
examples: Dropping a stone from some height; all bodies fall with same g in absence of air resistance.

concepts.title: Mass and Weight
concepts.definition: Mass is quantity of matter and constant at a place; weight is gravitational force on an object and can change with location.
examples: An object’s mass is same on Earth and Moon, but its weight on Moon is about one‑sixth of that on Earth.

concepts.title: Thrust, Pressure and Buoyancy (Concept)
concepts.definition: Thrust is force acting on a surface; pressure is thrust per unit area; fluids exert upward buoyant force on immersed bodies.
examples: Broad feet of camels reduce pressure on sand; objects feel lighter in water due to upthrust.

concepts.title: Archimedes’ Principle (Intro)
concepts.definition: When a body is partially or fully immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of fluid displaced.
examples: Ships made of iron float because their average density is less than water due to hollow structure.

concepts.title: Work Done by a Force
concepts.definition: Work is done when a force acts on an object and the object moves in the direction of force.
formulae: W = F × s (for constant force and motion in same direction)
examples: Lifting a bag from floor to table involves work done against gravity.

concepts.title: Forms of Energy and Kinetic Energy
concepts.definition: Energy is capacity to do work; kinetic energy is energy of motion.
examples: A moving car has kinetic energy; faster motion or greater mass means more kinetic energy.

concepts.title: Potential Energy
concepts.definition: Potential energy is energy possessed by a body due to its position or configuration, such as gravitational potential energy.
formulae: Potential energy Ep = m g h (near Earth’s surface)
examples: Water stored at height in a dam has potential energy.

concepts.title: Work–Energy Theorem (Concept)
concepts.definition: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy.
examples: If a car speeds up, positive work by engine increases its kinetic energy.

concepts.title: Law of Conservation of Energy
concepts.definition: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only changed from one form to another; total energy of an isolated system remains constant.
examples: In a swinging pendulum, potential energy converts to kinetic and back; total remains constant ignoring air resistance.

concepts.title: Production and Propagation of Sound
concepts.definition: Sound is produced by vibrating objects and travels in a medium as longitudinal waves of compression and rarefaction.
examples: Vibrating strings of guitar, vocal cords in humans produce sound.

concepts.title: Speed of Sound and Medium
concepts.definition: Speed of sound depends on medium; it is fastest in solids, slower in liquids and slowest in gases.
examples: Sound travels faster in steel than in air.

concepts.title: Wavelength, Frequency and Time Period
concepts.definition: Wavelength is distance between successive compressions or rarefactions; frequency is number of complete vibrations per second; time period is time for one vibration.
examples: A tuning fork vibrating 256 times per second has frequency 256 Hz.

concepts.title: Relation Between Speed, Frequency and Wavelength
concepts.definition: The speed of a sound wave equals product of its frequency and wavelength.
examples: If speed of sound is 340 m/s and frequency is 170 Hz, wavelength is 2 m.

concepts.title: Characteristics of Sound and Noise Pollution
concepts.definition: Loudness depends on amplitude, pitch on frequency and quality on waveform. Unwanted, unpleasant sounds are noise; too much noise causes noise pollution and health issues.
examples: High‑pitched whistle has high frequency; loud music at high volume causes noise pollution.

concepts.title: Need for Improving Food Resources
concepts.definition: Growing population and limited land area make it necessary to improve both quantity and quality of food produced.
examples: Higher‑yielding and disease‑resistant crop varieties help produce more food per hectare.

concepts.title: Crop Variety Improvement
concepts.definition: Developing better crop varieties with higher yield, better quality, disease resistance and tolerance to stresses using hybridisation and selection.
examples: Developing wheat varieties resistant to rust disease and suitable for different climates.

concepts.title: Crop Production and Management
concepts.definition: Improved crop production uses better agronomic practices, adequate nutrient and water supply and weed and pest control.
examples: Timely irrigation, balanced use of fertilisers and manures and proper crop rotation improve yields.

concepts.title: Animal Husbandry
concepts.definition: Scientific management and breeding of animals like cattle, poultry, fish and bees to increase production of milk, meat, eggs, fish and honey.
examples: Cross‑breeding high‑milk‑yield foreign cattle with local breeds; poultry farming for layers and broilers; beekeeping near orchards.
